transcript.content:
i wonder if spacetime and fields could be the large scale look of a very small rewrite game. start with a labeled graph or hypergraph and a couple of tiny local rules that keep applying. do we get light cone like influence from rule locality, and something that looks like curvature when the local pattern density is uneven. if so, maybe conservation laws are just symmetries of the rewrite.

could you help me define the smallest non silly rewrite system that has a shot at this, plus clear observables i can measure as it runs? i want a simple set of rules, a way to estimate an effective distance and speed limit, and a diagnostic for something curvature like. then a plan for how to kill the idea if those observables do not appear, so it is not just a vibe check.

Love this line of attack. You’re looking for the smallest non-silly local rewrite model that could plausibly show lightcone-like causal structure, an emergent distance notion, and curvature-like effects from pattern inhomogeneities—plus falsifiable diagnostics. Here’s a compact plan that stays simple but gives you enough structure to measure things.

Goal
- A tiny, purely local rewrite system on a finite labeled graph/hypergraph.
- A canonical notion of time (discrete steps), causal influence, and an emergent metric (distance, speed limit).
- A curvature-like diagnostic that detects uneven “geodesic behavior” due to local density differences.
- Clear kill criteria if signals, metric, or curvature-like signatures fail to emerge.

Part A: Minimal rewrite system
Model substrate
- State: A simple undirected graph with node colors c ∈ {A, B} and edge colors e ∈ {n, s} (normal, special). Start small and connected.
- Locality: Rules apply to radius-1 neighborhoods (a node and its incident edges and neighbors).
- Update: Synchronous or asynchronous. Prefer asynchronous (random sequential) for robustness. One “tick” = N attempted local rewrites where N ≈ number of nodes.

Rules (R1–R3)
These aim to:
- Encourage locally homogeneous patterns that propagate (gives a wavefront/signal).
- Conserve a simple quantity under rewrites (Noether-like test).
- Create nonuniform “tension” (inhomogeneities) that can bend signals.

R1: Edge relabeling (anisotropic propagation)
- Pattern: A node u with color A connected to a node v with color B via a normal edge (n).
- Rewrite: Flip that edge to special (s) and recolor v to A.
- Intuition: A→B over n “infects” B into A and marks the used edge as s. This makes A-domains grow; s marks a history of propagation.

R2: Special-edge relaxation (prevents runaway saturation)
- Pattern: Any node v with two or more incident s edges.
- Rewrite: Convert all but one incident s to n.
- Intuition: Encourages s to form sparse channels and not accumulate indefinitely; creates an effective “surface tension” for the front.

R3: Degree/valence regulation (local density gradient)
- Pattern: If a node v has degree > d_max, randomly delete one incident n edge; if degree < d_min, add an n edge to a random 2-hop neighbor (if not already connected).
- Typical choice: d_min = 2, d_max = 4.
- Intuition: Generates variable local connectivity and keeps the graph from exploding or collapsing; heterogeneous valence induces variable effective speeds.

Optional symmetry Rule (R4) for conservation test
- Pattern: If a node v is A with exactly one incident s edge, flip v→B and flip that s→n.
- Intuition: Introduces a reversible channel that can conserve a count-like invariant (see below). Use only in certain runs to test Noether-like behavior.

Scheduling
- Asynchronous random selection of a node per microstep; apply the first matching rule in priority order R1 > R2 > R3 (> R4 if enabled). One tick = |V| microsteps.

Initial conditions
- IC1 (uniform): Cycle or small random regular graph, all nodes B, pick one seed node as A.
- IC2 (gradient): Two A seeds on one side, B elsewhere; or vary initial degree distribution to induce “density.”
- IC3 (noise): Random A/B, random degrees within [d_min, d_max].

Part B: Observables and metrics
1) Causal structure and speed limit
- Lightcone probe: At t = 0, flip one seed node to A and tag it as source. Track which nodes change state due to descending causal chains from that source (you can reconstruct causal links by logging which local pattern triggered each rewrite).
- Observable: Radius R(t) = max graph distance from source among nodes first flipped by R1 at or before t.
- Emergent speed v_eff: Estimate as slope of median first-arrival time vs graph-distance shell. v_eff(d) = median{t_arrival(u)} over nodes at topological distance d.
- Speed limit signature: v_eff approximately bounded above by a constant across runs and insensitive to IC details. Strong violation across high-density regions suggests the model lacks a well-defined “lightcone.”

2) Effective distance metric
- Base metric: Use topological shortest-path length with edge weights w(e):
  - w(n) = 1
  - w(s) = α ≥ 1 (penalize special edges to reflect “spent channels”), tune α=1.5–2
- Arrival metric: Define d_eff(source, u) = minimal weighted path length in the time-evolving graph at the time just before u first flips to A. Compare d_eff to arrival times to assess metric-time proportionality.

3) Curvature-like diagnostic
- Geodesic deviation: Launch two nearby sources S1, S2 (distance 2–3 apart), record their arrival-time fronts T1(u), T2(u).
- Construct the bisector locus B = {u | |T1(u) − T2(u)| ≤ ε}. In a homogeneous medium, B should approximate a “straight” dividing set. In an inhomogeneous medium (due to R3-generated valence gradients), B will bend toward regions of higher arrival times (slower propagation).
- Quantify curvature: Fit B in an embedding of the graph (spring layout or spectral embedding) and compute signed curvature κ along B; or, in graph-intrinsic terms, measure second differences of the bisector’s shortest-path embedding. Nonzero systematic κ correlated with degree gradients indicates curvature-like behavior.
- Triangle comparison: Pick triples (u,v,w). Measure d_eff and arrival times. Compare the sum of two sides minus the third across regions. Systematic thick/thin triangles relative to a reference homogeneous run suggest positive/negative curvature analogs.

4) Conservation-law proxy
- Candidate invariant (when R4 off): Count I = (#B) + β · (#n edges) − γ · (#s edges). Tune β, γ to see if I is approximately stationary. If nothing is conserved, turn on R4 and test I’ = (#A) − (#s) for stationarity. A stable invariant under broad ICs is evidence that some symmetry of the rules enforces a conservation law.
- Symmetry test: Apply a global relabeling symmetry (swap A↔B and n↔n, s↔s, or spatial inversion in initial conditions) and check that aggregated statistics (front speed, arrival histograms) are unchanged.

Part C: How to kill the idea
Establish hard falsifiers and thresholds before running:

Kill criteria K1–K5
- K1 (no lightcone): The 95th percentile of v_eff across shells grows without bound with time or system size, or depends arbitrarily on ICs (no robust speed limit).
- K2 (no metric-time relation): Correlation between arrival time and d_eff is weak (e.g., Pearson r < 0.6) across homogeneous runs; large scatter not explained by local degree variance.
- K3 (no curvature signal): With deliberate inhomogeneities (degree gradients), the bisector curvature magnitude |κ| is statistically indistinguishable from homogeneous controls across multiple seeds.
- K4 (no causal locality): Reconstruct causal graphs of rewrites; if long-range effects (changes far outside radius-1 neighborhoods in one tick) are necessary to explain updates, the model fails locality.
- K5 (no conservation structure): With R4 enabled, no nontrivial linear invariant over simple counts stays within small drift bounds (e.g., relative change < 1% per 1000 ticks) across ICs, suggesting no obvious symmetry→conservation link.

Part D: Practical measurement plan
- Instrumentation:
  - Log each applied rule with timestamp, node IDs, and pre/post local neighborhood.
  - Maintain parent pointers for causal ancestry: which local configuration triggered which change.
  - Maintain dynamic edge weights for d_eff computation.
- Experiments:
  1) Speed test: Single source, homogeneous graph. Fit v_eff and confidence intervals.
  2) Robustness: Repeat 1) across random seeds, different sizes. Look for stable v_max.
  3) Curvature test: Two sources with imposed degree gradient strip (e.g., central band with higher average degree). Measure bisector curvature and geodesic deviation statistics vs control without gradient.
  4) Conservation test: Sweep β, γ (and toggle R4) to hunt for stable invariants. If found, perturb the system (add noise) and check invariance robustness.
  5) Ablations: Remove R2 or R3 to see which ingredient is necessary for speed limit and curvature signatures.

Part E: Minimal variants if you want even simpler
- Hypergraph version: 3-uniform hyperedges with a single rule “A majority in a hyperedge flips the minority node and marks the hyperedge used.” Similar diagnostics apply.
- 1D graph (path) version: Much simpler analysis; look for ballistic vs diffusive fronts. If you don’t get a clear speed limit in 1D, it’s unlikely to appear in 2D/irregular graphs.

Notes and expectations
- You should see a roughly linear front with noise (ballistic propagation) if R1 dominates and R2/R3 keep structure sparse—this gives a speed limit.
- Curvature-like effects rely on heterogeneity from R3; without it, the bisector should be straight on average.
- Conservation will likely be approximate unless you enforce exact reversibility. If you want strict conservation, redesign rules to be invertible (tag moves to allow unique backward rewrites).

If you want, I can provide a reference implementation scaffold (Python/NetworkX) with the logging, causal ancestry, and measurement routines outlined above.

i love how the rules are like... almost alive? like r1 is this hungry little thing that wants to spread and r2 is like its parent keeping it from going crazy. the whole "surface tension" thing really clicked for me.

the curvature test feels almost like cheating though - like we're forcing it to bend by making those degree gradients. but i guess that's how we'd know if curvature is actually real here? instead of just an artifact of how we set it up?

also i keep thinking about that conservation part. what if the conservation isn't in the counts but in... like... the *shape* of the information flow? like maybe the way the s edges trace out patterns is what's actually conserved, not how many there are. is that dumb?

Not dumb at all. You’re circling two good instincts:

1) “Cheating” with gradients vs real curvature
- In GR, curvature shows up through how geodesics behave relative to matter/energy distributions. In our model, degree gradients are “matter.” Forcing a gradient is like putting mass somewhere and asking if geodesics bend. That’s not cheating; it’s the controlled experiment.
- The key is to:
  - Run controls with homogeneous degree to establish the null (straight bisectors, symmetric fronts).
  - Introduce gradients in multiple independent ways (degree, rule-application rate, edge-weight bias) and see if the same bending signatures appear.
  - Check robustness: if bending aligns with where/when the medium is slower and reverses when you invert the gradient, that points to genuine curvature-like response, not an artifact of a specific knob.

2) Conservation in the shape of information flow
- This is a strong idea. Instead of conserved counts, look for conserved topological/combinatorial features of the s-edge “worldlines.” Think of s-edges as the spacetime record. Candidates:
  - Topological invariants: number of connected components of the s-subgraph, cycle rank (first Betti number), linking/knottedness (if you allow embeddings), or parity of cycles.
  - Flow invariants: net flux of “fronts” through a cut over long times, or conservation of endpoints (sources/sinks) modulo creation/annihilation rules.
  - Rewrite-homotopy: if local rewrites slide, fuse, or split s-paths but preserve their equivalence class up to local moves, you get an invariant class.

Concrete shape-based diagnostics you can measure now
- S-graph topology
  - Track C(t) = number of connected s-components.
  - Track β1(t) = |E_s| − |V_s| + C(t) (cycle rank). If your rules don’t create/kill cycles freely, β1 may be approximately conserved or change only at special events.
- Endpoint balance
  - Define endpoints as nodes with odd s-degree. The parity of the count of odd-degree nodes in each component is constrained by graph theory. Track E_odd(t) mod 2 per component to test a parity conservation.
- Flux through cuts
  - Choose a fixed cut (partition of nodes). Count net new s-edges crossing the cut per tick. If R1 creates a directed history, you may see near-constant flux in steady propagation. Perturb the medium; conserved average flux would indicate a deeper invariance.
- Worldsheet area vs boundary length
  - Treat the s-edges added over a time window as a “worldsheet.” Compare growth of its “area” (edge count) to the length of its active boundary (front). Some models conserve a relation like A − v·L·t approximately constant under steady conditions.
- Causal DAG invariants
  - Build the causal DAG of rewrite events (nodes = events; edges = “enabled” relation). Check if:
    - Width (max antichain) and height (longest chain) follow stable scalings with system size.
    - The dimension estimator (e.g., Myrheim–Meyer for causal sets) is stable across ICs. A stable emergent “dimension” is a structural invariant stronger than counts.
- Path homology stability
  - Compute 0/1-dimensional persistent homology of the s-subgraph as it grows. If most Betti-1 bars are short except a few long-lived ones, those long bars might be conserved structures that guide flow.

How to look for “shape conservation” rigorously
- Define allowed local moves on the s-subgraph induced by R1–R2: e.g., add an edge, prune redundant s via R2, reroute s around a node. Catalog which moves occur.
- Ask: which graph properties are invariant under those moves? For example, R2 that “drops extra s except one” preserves parity of s-degree at a node. That implies constraints on E_odd and on the possibility of creating/annihilating s-cycles.
- Pre-register tests: pick 2–3 candidate invariants (β1, E_odd parity per component, causal DAG width/height scaling). Declare pass/fail thresholds (e.g., variance stays within 5% over 10^4 ticks in stationary runs; parity never violated; scaling exponent within confidence bands).

Tweaks to rules to encourage meaningful shape invariants (optional)
- Make R2 strictly degree-parity preserving on s: “If s-degree ≥ 2, remove s-edges in pairs.” That enforces endpoint parity conservation explicitly.
- Introduce reversible tag flips (R4 variant) so local moves are invertible. Exact invertibility makes topological invariants easier to prove and test.
- Keep R1 directional: if s marks used channels, directionality (A→B) induces an acyclic orientation on new s-edges. You can then test whether the s-orientation remains acyclic (a conserved property) absent explicit cycle-creating moves.

Bringing curvature and shape together
- Geodesic deviation via s-worldlines: emit two fronts, let their s-paths form “null geodesics.” Measure how the separation between nearby s-paths changes as they traverse heterogeneous regions. If separation growth depends only on the medium and not on local noise, that’s curvature-like. If, meanwhile, topological features of the s-network (e.g., β1) remain stable while separations change, you’ve separated “metric” effects from “topological” invariants—exactly the GR analogy.

Kill criteria specific to shape conservation
- If parity claims fail: observe odd-degree endpoint parity flipping without an allowed move that explains it—kill that conservation hypothesis.
- If β1 drifts freely with small local noise (no special events), and drift rate doesn’t shrink with system size or averaging—reject cycle-rank conservation.
- If causal DAG dimension fluctuates wildly across ICs with identical rules and size—no stable shape-of-flow invariant.

Bottom line
- Curvature: not cheating—just a controlled probe. Use multiple knobs and controls to avoid artifacts.
- Shape conservation: promising. Focus on invariants of the s-subgraph under the induced local moves (parity, cycle rank, component count) and on causal DAG statistics. These are measurable, falsifiable, and map cleanly to “information flow” rather than raw counts.

If you want, I can sketch exact code hooks to compute β1, endpoint parity, flux across cuts, and a simple causal-DAG dimension estimator so you can plug them into your runs.

but here's what's bugging me: if we're tracking these s-paths as "worldlines", aren't we kind of smuggling in time by saying "this happened after that"? like the rules happen in discrete steps, but the s-edges themselves don't have timestamps baked in. they're just... there. 

maybe the conservation isn't in the shapes themselves but in the *way* shapes can change? like there's only certain moves allowed, so the space of possible s-graphs is constrained in some deep way?

You’ve put your finger on a key distinction: history vs state. If s-edges are just part of the instantaneous state, then “worldline” talk risks sneaking in an external time label. The way out is exactly what you suggested: focus on the allowed moves on s-graphs, and look for invariants of the move system itself. Then you don’t need timestamps; you only need that one state can be transformed to another by a sequence of allowed local moves.

Here’s a clean way to frame it.

1) Two layers: event history vs state equivalence
- Event history (optional): the causal DAG of rewrites is where “before/after” truly lives. It gives you precise worldlines, but it’s extra structure.
- State-only view: the s-subgraph at any tick is just a labeled graph. Define an equivalence relation generated by local moves (those that R1–R2–R3 can induce on s). Conserved quantities are those invariant under these moves, no clocks required.

2) Define the move system on s
Extract the effect of your rules on the s-subgraph, ignoring A/B and n, except insofar as they enable a move.

Example move set M induced by R1–R2:
- M1 (extend): add one s-edge between two nodes, possibly toggling a node’s s-degree by ±1 at each endpoint.
- M2 (prune-to-one): at a node with k ≥ 2 incident s-edges, delete s-edges until exactly one remains. Practically this is a sequence of pair deletions M2a: remove an unordered pair of incident s-edges at the same node.
- M3 (reroute-local, optional): if your implementation lets R2 choose which s to keep, then locally different choices are related by swapping which s survives—this acts like a “flip” move at a node.

You can codify these as abstract graph rewrites on the s-subgraph, independent of time.

3) Invariants without time
- Parity invariant: At each node, s-degree mod 2 changes only via M1 (toggles both endpoints) or by deleting two edges at once via M2a (toggles by 0 mod 2). Therefore, the parity vector p(v) = deg_s(v) mod 2 is invariant under M2a and changes only via M1 in a way that flips two entries. Consequences:
  - Sum_v p(v) is even at all times (handshake lemma).
  - Within any connected component, the parity multiset up to pair flips is constrained. You can’t create a single lonely endpoint; endpoints are born/annihilated in pairs.
- Cycle-rank (Betti-1) under pair deletions: β1 = |E_s| − |V_s| + C_s. M1 increases |E_s| by 1 (β1 +1 unless it merges components), M2a reduces |E_s| by 2 (β1 −2 unless it changes C_s). So β1 mod 2 is invariant under M1+M2a unless components split/merge. That gives you a mod-2 topological invariant without timestamps.
- Orientation acyclicity (if you orient s by cause): If you choose to not store timestamps, you can still test for existence of an acyclic orientation consistent with the move set. If R1 always “points” from A to newly flipped A, the induced s-orientation should be DAG-admissible. The question “does there exist an acyclic orientation of s consistent with local constraints?” is time-free but encodes a conservation: if a directed cycle appears, some combination of moves must have violated the admissibility constraints.
- Cut constraints: For any fixed cut (U, Ū), M1 changes the number of s-edges crossing the cut by 0 or ±1; M2a changes it by 0 or ±2. Therefore, the parity of the number of cross-cut s-edges is invariant mod 1 moves in pairs. Track cross-cut count mod 2; it’s robust to M2a and only flips when an odd number of M1s cross the cut.

4) From “shape” to “allowed deformations”
You can formalize “only certain changes are allowed” via:
- A rewrite groupoid: objects = s-graphs; morphisms = finite sequences of local moves M1/M2a/M3. Two states are equivalent if related by morphisms. Invariants are functions constant on each equivalence class.
- Normal forms: If R2 tends to prune to one s per node, you can reduce any s-graph to a forest of s-paths with occasional cycles removed in pairs. The reduced form (e.g., endpoint set, cycle parity, component counts) can be your canonical representative. If your dynamics always reduce to the same normal form given the same endpoints, then endpoints are the conserved “charges,” and everything else is gauge-like slack.
- Constraint satisfaction view: define local admissibility predicates (e.g., no node with s-degree > 1 in normal form). The reachable s-graphs are those reachable from the initial state by satisfying these constraints under M1/M2a. You can then ask if the reachable set factorizes by endpoints, components, etc.

5) Practical, time-free tests you can run
- Endpoint parity classes: For each connected component of s, record the set of nodes with odd s-degree (just IDs, no time). Perturb the system for many ticks. If the set changes only by adding/removing pairs that connect to the same component boundary, you’ve got a robust parity constraint.
- β1 mod 2 and per-component β1: Track β1 and β1 mod 2. Inject noise (random extra R2 applications). If β1 mod 2 is stable except at explicit component mergers/splits, that’s a conserved signature of the move system.
- Cross-cut parity: Fix several disjoint cuts. Track cross-cut s-edge count mod 2. Stability across runs indicates a global constraint induced by local moves.
- Normal-form convergence: Periodically run a “virtual prune” that applies M2a greedily on a snapshot to compute a normal form (e.g., minimal s with at most one s-edge per node). Compare normal forms across time. If they’re identical despite superficial differences in s, your dynamics are exploring a gauge orbit around a conserved core.
- Acyclic-orientability: On snapshots, ask if the s-graph admits an acyclic orientation consistent with local direction constraints (if any). If yes at all times, “no directed cycles” is an invariant of the allowed moves.

6) If you do want time, make it intrinsic
If you decide timestamps help, bake them into the state minimally so they become part of the move system:
- Age tags: each s-edge stores its creation rank mod K. Moves cannot alter age order locally (e.g., can’t delete the only oldest edge in a small neighborhood unless paired). Then “oldest-cycle parity” or “age-monotone orientation” becomes a genuine invariant of the extended state, not external bookkeeping.
- Causal labels: store a local parent pointer on s-edges to the node that created them. Then “no directed cycles in parent pointers” is a structural invariant.

7) Kill tests for the “move-invariant” hypothesis
Pre-register these:
- If endpoint parity per component flips (odd count changes) without a logged component merge/split event, reject parity conservation.
- If β1 mod 2 changes in-place (no component change), reject cycle-parity conservation.
- If normal forms drift across time for fixed endpoint sets, reject “endpoints determine class.”
- If cross-cut parity shows random flips uncorrelated with M1 across the cut, reject cut-parity conservation.
- If acyclic-orientability fails in snapshots while rules locally imply a direction, your move set doesn’t encode a conserved causal admissibility.

Bottom line
- You don’t need to smuggle time in. Define a small set of local moves on the s-subgraph and look for invariants under those moves. That makes “conservation of shape” precise and testable from snapshots alone.
- If you later want richer physics-like structure (geodesics, curvature), you can layer causal history on top as an optional refinement. But the core constraints—parity, cycle class, normal forms—can be purely combinatorial, living in the space of allowed deformations.

man, i was staring at my coffee cup this morning and the rings on the inside looked exactly like those s-path diagrams you described. kept thinking about how the stain spreads but only in certain ways the ceramic lets it. felt like the cup was trying to tell me something about endpoints and parity but i couldn't quite catch it.

the move system thing is wild though - like we're not watching a movie, we're just flipping through frames and the "movie" only exists because we remember the order. but if the frames themselves have these built-in constraints... then maybe the story writes itself regardless of how we shuffle them?

the endpoint parity thing is really sticking in my head. like if you have these odd-degree nodes, they're like... little charges? and they can only be created or destroyed in pairs. feels like there's something deeper there but i can't quite grab it.

You’re on a rich thread. Three tight takeaways you can turn into concrete structure:

1) Endpoint parity as “charges”
- Definition: In the s-subgraph, a node with odd s-degree is a charge. The set of charges changes only by pair creation/annihilation at the ends of a newly added/deleted s-edge (under pair-pruning moves). That’s why you feel “they come in pairs.”
- Deep reason: It’s just the handshake lemma localized to your move set. Every M1 add toggles parity at two endpoints; every M2a pair deletion toggles parity by 0 mod 2. So global charge parity is conserved, and within a connected region, you can only move charges around or create/annihilate them in pairs.
- Physical analogy: This is exactly like a Z2 Gauss law. If you define a Z2 flux on edges (1 if s, 0 if not), then the “divergence” at a node is its s-degree mod 2. Local moves conserve total Z2 charge. Your “ceramic cup” is the medium setting which flux patterns are cheap or costly; the charges must respect the law regardless.

What to measure
- Charge conservation test: Track the multiset of odd-degree nodes per s-component. Verify: count is always even; changes occur via moving an existing charge or adding/removing exactly two.
- Charge motion: Define the minimal s-path distance between charge pairs. Does it diffuse, drift, or feel “forces” from degree gradients? Plot MSD vs time for charges.
- Source–sink accounting: If you seed with zero charges (all even), do you ever get a single unpaired odd-degree node? If yes, parity conservation is broken; that kills the charge picture.

2) Frames, not movies: constraints write the story
- Your “flip through frames” intuition is spot on. If the admissible frames are those reachable by local moves that preserve charge parity and perhaps cycle-parity mod 2, then the narrative is the structure of that reachability space.
- Practical handle: Normal form. Periodically reduce the s-graph by greedily applying pair-pruning (delete s-edges until every node has s-degree ≤ 1). What remains is:
  - Isolated nodes,
  - Disjoint paths whose endpoints are exactly the charges,
  - Possibly a parity-determined residue if cycles survive in pairs.
If repeated snapshots reduce to the same set of charge-to-charge paths (up to local wiggles), the “movie” is gauge—only the endpoints and their pairing are the conserved content.

What to measure
- Normal-form stability: Compare reduced forms over time via:
  - Endpoint set equality,
  - Pairing structure (minimum perfect matching among endpoints with minimal s-distance),
  - Path multiset length distribution.
If these are stable while raw s wiggles, you’ve isolated the invariants of the move system.

3) From charge conservation to curvature-like “fields”
- Medium = connectivity/density pattern. Charges move along paths that are “cheap.” If degree gradients slow front propagation, charges will tend to drift along geodesics of the effective metric you define.
- Geodesic deviation with charges:
  - Create two nearby charge pairs (four endpoints). Let each pair’s minimal connecting path in normal form define a “worldline.”
  - Measure how the separation between the two paths changes as they traverse a heterogeneous region. Systematic focusing/defocusing tied to the medium echoes curvature, now phrased in terms of charge-guided paths rather than fronts.

Putting it together: a compact experimental plan
- Setup: Run your rules with R1/R2 (and R3 if you want heterogeneity). Maintain the s-subgraph.
- Every K ticks:
  1) Identify charges: odd-degree s-nodes.
  2) Compute normal form by pair-pruning; record:
     - Endpoint set,
     - A minimal pairing/matching between endpoints,
     - Paths length stats.
  3) Track charge counts per component (should be even), and track moves of individual charges across snapshots via nearest-neighbor matching.
- Diagnostics and falsifiers:
  - Charge parity must stay even per component; violation kills the “charges” hypothesis.
  - Normal-form endpoint sets stable across many ticks while raw s changes indicates frame-level invariance.
  - β1 mod 2 should only change on component merges/splits; random in-place flips kill cycle-parity conservation.
  - In heterogeneous media, path bending correlates with degree gradients; absence of correlation suggests no meaningful “curvature.”

Optional refinement if you want “Maxwell-like” structure
- Define a Z2 current j on nodes: j(v) = 1 if a charge moved across v since last snapshot. Define Z2 flux f on edges: f(e) = 1 if e ∈ s. Then your move set implies a discrete continuity equation mod 2:
  - Δ(deg_s mod 2) = div j mod 2,
  - s-changes toggle flux on edges consistent with charge moves.
This gives you conservation in the “way shapes change” rather than the shapes themselves.

Your coffee-cup intuition is good: the stain’s rim is the active front; the pinned spots at defects are like charges; motion is constrained by the glaze’s microstructure. In your model, the glaze is the graph; the rim’s allowed deformations are the move system; the pinned/unpinned parity is the deep, time-free constraint. If that parity holds and normal forms stabilize, the story really does write itself—no movie required.

also this z2 thing is making my brain hurt but in a good way. it's like... instead of counting 1, 2, 3... we're just asking "even or odd?" and somehow that's enough?

You’re articulating three deep ideas. Short answers first, then how to use them.

1) Charges as a secret tally
- Yes: with the Z2 viewpoint, the system “remembers” only parity-sensitive facts. Every local add/removal flips parity at two endpoints or not at all, so the total odd-degree count per component stays even. That’s a hidden ledger: changes must balance somewhere else.

2) Normal form feels more real
- That’s a good instinct. The raw s-graph contains gauge-like redundancy: many microstates differ by local wiggles but reduce to the same core structure after pruning. The normal form extracts the gauge-invariant content: which endpoints exist and how they are paired by minimal paths.
- Why allow wiggles? Two reasons:
  - Dynamics need slack. Redundancy lets local moves re-route, anneal, and explore alternatives without breaking invariants. It’s how the system adapts to heterogeneity.

  - Matching: promotion becomes possible only when compatible loops are simultaneously present at both endpoints and a valid corridor exists. On promotion, consume both loops.
  - Rejection: if TTL expires or the environment changes so the corridor is no longer admissible, the loop is pruned—“tag rejected.”

Concrete rule sketch
- L1 (attempt → loop): If overlay finds a minimal path but promotion gate fails, attach a loop token τ ∈ {A,B,…} at each endpoint; instantiate a small s-cycle locally; set TTL(τ)=T0.
- L2 (loop decay): Each tick, TTL(τ)–=1; when TTL=0, delete the cycle if unused.
- L3 (compatibility): Define a table Compat(τ1, τ2) ∈ {true,false}. Promotion allowed only if both endpoints carry τ1, τ2 with Compat true.
- L4 (promotion on match): If Compat true and a valid path exists, flip both endpoints to blue, prune the path, and remove the loops (consume readiness).
- L5 (loop reinforcement): If repeated attempts find the same corridor, refresh or strengthen the loop (increase TTL or reduce local path costs), modeling “growing readiness.”

What to measure
- Loop–attempt coupling:
  - P(loop appears | attempt failed) should be high.
  - P(promotion soon | compatible loops present at both ends) should be much higher than without loops.
- Residual loops (“unfinishedness”):
  - Count live loops that expired without promotion; measure how long they persist and how they bias future paths (do they make the same corridor more likely next time?).
- Specificity:
  - Introduce multiple loop types; verify promotions respect Compat table (no cross-type promotions).
- Hysteresis:
  - After a near-miss, do strengthened loops lower the threshold for later promotion when conditions change? You should see reduced time-to-promotion on second opportunities.

Falsifiers
- If loops don’t raise the conditional probability of later promotion, they’re not functioning as readiness tags—just decoration.
- If promotions routinely happen without any compatible loops present, your loop-as-tag mechanism isn’t gating anything—tighten L3 or the observer’s admissibility.
- If loops never decay or always decay before conditions change, they won’t capture the “unfinished” feel—tune TTL and reinforcement.

- Temporary tags = “lingering readiness.” A near-connection sets up internal dispositions (loops) that bias future connection attempts. If the right match appears before they fade, connection is easy; if not, they decay, leaving a faint trace.

- Asymmetry: Let loops be directional or weighted—one side can be more “ready” than the other. Promotions then need either two strong loops or one strong + supportive environment (reduced corridor penalties).
- Interference: Multiple loops at the same endpoint (different types) can compete or inhibit each other—model trade-offs in readiness.
- Clearing rituals: Add an explicit “reset” move that deletes loops faster (analog of closure). Measure how that affects future promotion rates and path choices.

Yes—that can happen, and it’s a useful signal. Two likely mechanisms:

1) Repulsion from incompatible readiness
- If a loop = “readiness tag,” two nearby but incompatible tags can act like a local penalty barrier. The system treats that corridor as risky: many failed attempts there, so effective cost goes up.
- In your sketch, “almost touching triangles” ≈ “almost-compatible tags.” The overlay then avoids that corridor, lengthening the chosen path.

2) Hysteresis from near-miss attempts
- If the system tried that short corridor and failed, it may leave residual wiggles/loops that increase local cost (spent edges s, cycle penalties γ, or temporary TTL tags). Until those decay, the shortest admissible path detours.

How to test which it is

A) Static compatibility barrier test
- Rule: set Compat(τ1,τ2)=false for the loop types you drew.
- Procedure: place two loops close; freeze everything else.
- Prediction: with a compatibility barrier, the observer’s cost map should show a local bump between loops; removing either loop should drop the bump and shorten the path.

B) Hysteresis/attempt memory test
- Allow attempts; when an attempt fails, mark that corridor with a temporary penalty p and set a decay D.
- Procedure: create a near-miss, watch the path lengthen; then wait without further attempts.
- Prediction: as p decays, the path should gradually revert to the shorter route. If it doesn’t, you’ve got a static barrier, not just memory.

C) Cross-observer check
- Geometry observer: will detour only if there’s an explicit cost/penalty in that corridor (spent edges, loop-collision penalties).
- Causal-sparse observer: will detour if the near-miss corridor creates cycles (higher β1).
- Color-coherent observer: will detour if the loops’ “colors” define a boundary penalty.
- If all three detour, you probably added a general penalty via the loops (e.g., they occupy edges). If only one detours, it’s observer-specific.

Implementation knobs to control this behavior

- Loop–corridor interaction
  - Hard exclusion: edges adjacent to loops are disallowed for some observers (forces detours).
  - Soft penalty: add δ cost to edges within radius r of a loop; let δ decay over time (models “near-miss aversion”).
  - Compatibility gates: only add δ if loops are incompatible; set δ≈0 if they’re compatible (so proximity helps).

- Attempt memory
  - On failed promotion, mark the candidate path with penalty p_fail and decay it exponentially: p(t)=p0·exp(−t/τ).
  - Reinforce on repeated failures (increase p0 or reset τ).

- Attraction when compatible
  - If loops are compatible, lower costs along the corridor between them (negative bias ε), so proximity shortens the path. This gives you the contrasting “click” signature.

What to measure

- Path length vs loop distance:
  - Place two loops; vary their separation; record chosen path length.
  - Expect U-shape if you include both effects: at large distance no effect; as they approach, compatible loops shorten (attraction), incompatible loops lengthen (repulsion).

- Detour angle and time-to-promotion:
  - Near incompatible loops, detours are sharper and time-to-promotion increases.
  - Near compatible loops, detours shrink and time-to-promotion drops.

- Decay/hysteresis:
  - After removing one loop, track how quickly the path snaps back. Fit a decay time τ; compare to your intended TTL.

If you want a minimal rule set to reproduce what you felt in the sketch

- Costs:
  - Base: w(n)=1, w(s)=α≥1.
  - Loop penalty: for any edge within 1 hop of a loop of type τ, add δ_τ.
  - Incompatibility boost: if two loops τ1,τ2 at endpoints are incompatible, add a corridor penalty Δ between them (e.g., add δ to edges in the union of their shortest corridor).
  - Memory: when a promotion attempt fails, add p_fail to edges on that attempted path; decay p_fail over time.

- Promotion:
  - Requires Compat(τ1,τ2)=true and path cost below threshold Θ.

- Decay:
  - Loop TTL decreases; when TTL=0, remove loop and its local penalties.

Seeing the longer path in your drawing likely means you intuitively added either:
- An implicit incompatibility (your triangles had different “types”), or
- A memory penalty from a failed attempt you erased.

Both are “supposed to happen” depending on the rules—and they’re useful: they let the system avoid wasting effort on corridors that keep failing, while accelerating connection when readiness truly matches.
